---
title: "Introduction to R for Data Analysis"
subtitle: "Workflow and Reproducibility"
author: "Jan Schwalbach & Dennis Abel"
date: "2023-08-18"
presenter: Jan
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---
layout: true 

```{r child = "./content/config/sessions_setup.Rmd"}
```
 
---

## Content of this session

- How to write good R code and scripts?

- Short introduction to R Projects

- A short outlook on more advanced coding

---


## Why a session on reproducibility?

.center[
```{r, out.width = "55%", echo = FALSE}
woRkshoptools::include_picture("old_code.jpg")
```
.footnote[[Source](https://www.reddit.com/media?url=https%3A%2F%2Fi.redd.it%2Fwhen-you-come-back-to-code-v0-66xav7amfsp91.jpg%3Fs%3D6b642442162f7421f1ec7082a46a16fe4604c4b1)]
]

---



## Goal
We want...

1) ... to understand our own code in the future

2) ... others to understand our code

That's why we care about...

1) ... script organization

2) ... code style

---


## Script Organization

- It is always better to write many small scripts within a project than one very, very large script

- If your script is getting longer and more complicated, use sections and subsections

- Start by loading the necessary libraries and packages. This section helps others understand the dependencies of your script.

- If you *finish* a script (e.g., when you submit it with a publication), provide information on the package versions you have used.

- Comments, comments, comments!


---

## Advanced commenting of `R` scripts

A neat trick in *RStudio* is that if you end a comment preceding a block of code with `####` (or more `#` or `----` or more dashes), you create a section in your script that can be collapsed and expanded in the *RStudio* script pane. This also creates a small interactive table of contents for the script at the bottom of the script pane. 

You can also use the keyboard shortcut <kbd>Ctrl + Shift + R</kbd> (*Windows* & *Linux*)/<kbd>Cmd + Shift + R</kbd> (*Mac*) to insert code sections.


While you can obviously introduce hierarchical orders through different names, it is not possible to integrate a folding hierarchy
```{r subsections}

### Subsection 1.1 ----

#### Subsection 1.1.1 ----

##### Subsection 1.1.1.1 ----
```


---

## Advanced commenting of `R` scripts

```{r, rstudio-scripts-expanded, out.width = "100%", echo=FALSE}
woRkshoptools::include_picture("./script_sections_expanded_highlighted.png")
```

---

## Advanced commenting of `R` scripts

```{r, rstudio_scripts_collapsed, out.width = "100%", echo=FALSE}
woRkshoptools::include_picture("./script_sections_collapsed_highlighted.png")
```

---

## Informative Headers

In order to get a fast overview, we recommend to include information like author, date, project, and the specific task in the header. Depending on your individual needs, this list can be extended (e.g., version...) 

```{r header}
################################################################
# Author:     Jan Schwalbach 
# Date:       July 2023
# Project:    ParlLawSpeech | OPTED
# Task:       Scraping Spanish speeches and transforming them
#             into a corpus and merging it with MP data (part 1)
################################################################

# Loading Packages
library(stringr)
library(rvest)
library(XML)
```

---

## Code Style

- Good coding style is like correct punctuation: you can manage without it, ... butitsuremakesthingseasiertoread.

- The goal of the *Style Guides* is to make our R code easier to read, share, and verify.

- As with writing texts, shitty first drafts are not bad. But the more you get used to writing with a good coding style, the easier it is

- We already touched upon the question of code style when talking about renaming variables (remember the snakes and camels? `r ji("snake")` `r ji("camel")`)

---


## Coding styles

What helps to increase the comprehensibility and reusability of code is to follow a specific coding style. There are quite a few things that can be regulated by coding style conventions, such as...
- line length (see the ["sacred 80 column rule"]( https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/EightyColumnRule))
- indentation
- variable/object naming
- comments
- assignment rules

`R` is pretty flexible in styling (compared, e.g., to `Python`, which has strict rules for indentation) which makes it even more necessary to think about these conventions.

---

## Coding styles

Two popular coding style guides for `R` are:

- [*Google*'s R style guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/Rguide.html)

- [the `tidyverse` style guide](https://style.tidyverse.org/)

---

## Coding in style `r ji("sunglasses")`
  
The [`styler`](http://styler.r-lib.org/) package, which includes an *RStudio* add-in, makes it easy to implement the `tidyverse` style guide.

```{r install & load styler, eval = F, echo = T}
install.packages("styler")
library(styler)

```

From the package documentation:
- `style_file()` styles .R and/or .Rmd files.
- `style_dir()` styles all .R and/or .Rmd files in a directory.

```{r, out.width = "50%", echo = FALSE}
woRkshoptools::include_picture("./styler_addin.png")
```

---



## *RStudio* projects

*RStudio* projects are helpful tool for developing a [project-oriented workflow](https://www.tidyverse.org/blog/2017/12/workflow-vs-script/) that can enhance reproducibility.

You already created a project via the *RStudio* menu: `File` -> `New Project`. *RStudio* projects are associated with `.Rproj` files that contain some specific settings for the project. If you double-click on a `.Rproj` file, this opens a new instance of *RStudio* with the working directory and file browser set to the location of that file (the repository/folder for this workshop contains an `.Rproj` file, if you want to try this out).

Explaining *RStudio* projects in detail is beyond the scope of this course, but there are good tutorials available, e.g., on the [*RStudio* support site](https://support.rstudio.com/hc/en-us/articles/200526207-Using-Projects) or in the [respective chapter in *What They Forgot to Teach You About R*](https://rstats.wtf/project-oriented-workflow.html#rstudio-projectsl).

---


## A short outlook on more advanced coding


If you plan to continue to work with `R` (something we would - again - always recommend `r emo::ji("stuck_out_tongue_winking_eye")`), you will come across some more advanced programming concepts. As this is an application-oriented introductory course, we do not want to confuse you with even more concepts and further possibilities that R offers. However, we do not want to hide them from you either as you will (hopefully) see them soon in code written by others...  

---


## Relational data

```{r libraries, include=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```


In some cases, you may need to combine different data sets (e.g., if you have separate data sets from waves of a longitudinal survey study or data on the same subjects from different sources). 

The simplest case is that the data frames you want to combine either have the same cases/rows in the same order but different variables/columns, or the same columns/variables (with the same names) but different cases/rows.

In those cases, you can use the `base R` functions `rbind()` to add/combine rows or `cbind()` to add/combine columns, or, better yet, `bind_rows()` or `bind_cols()` from `dplyr`.

---

## Relational data

For all other situations, you can use the `merge()` function from `base R`. However, a more convenient option is using the the so-called *two-table verbs* from `dplyr` which allow you to combine two (or more) tabular data sets in various ways. We will look at two categories of those verbs in the following:

1. Mutating joins  

2. Filtering joins  

If you have ever worked with (or at least seen) `SQL` code, many of the following things will look familiar. For a more in-depth introduction, you can have a look at the [chapter on relational data](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/relational-data.html) in *R for Data Science*.

---

## Superhero data `r ji("bat")``r ji("zap")``r ji("spider_web")``r ji("merman")`

As we cannot use the data from the *ALLBUS* 2021 data to illustrate the different joins from `dlpyr`, we will, instead, the [Super Heroes data set from *Kaggle*](https://www.kaggle.com/claudiodavi/superhero-set).

```{r superhero-data, include = F}
heroes <- read_csv("./data/heroes_information.csv") %>% 
  select(name, Alignment, Race, Publisher) %>% 
  rename(Name = name) %>%
  mutate(Publisher = str_replace(Publisher, " Comics", "")) %>%
    filter(
    (Name == "Batgirl" & Race != "-") |
      Name == "Aquaman" |
      Name == "Catwoman" |
      Name == "Magneto" |
      Name == "Deadpool" |
      Name == "Poison Ivy" |
      Name == "Hellboy"
  )

publishers <- read_csv("./data/heroes_information.csv") %>% 
  select(Publisher) %>%
  distinct() %>% 
  filter(
    Publisher == "Marvel Comics" |
    Publisher == "DC Comics" |
    Publisher == "Image Comics"
  ) %>% 
  distinct() %>%
  mutate(Publisher = str_replace(Publisher, " Comics", ""),
    Founded = case_when(
    Publisher == "Marvel" ~ 1939L,
    Publisher == "DC" ~ 1934L,
    Publisher == "Image" ~ 1992L
  ),
  Location = case_when(
    Publisher == "Marvel" ~ "NYC (NY)",
    Publisher == "DC" ~ "Burbank (CA)",
    Publisher == "Image" ~ "Berkeley (CA)"
  ))

```

.pull-left[
.center[
**Superheroes**
```{r superhero-table, echo = F}
heroes %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 16)
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.center[
**Publishers**
```{r publishers-table, echo = F}
publishers %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 16)
```
]
]

---

## Mutating joins

> A mutating join [...] .highlight[first matches observations by their keys], then .highlight[copies across variables from one table to the other] ([R for Data Science](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/relational-data.html#mutating-joins)).

- `inner_join()`  

- `left_join()`  

- `right_join()`  

- `full_join()`

---

## Inner join

 > All rows from `x` where there are matching values in `y`, and all columns from `x` and `y`.

```{r inner-gif, echo=FALSE, out.width="45%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/blob/main/images/inner-join.gif?raw=true")
```
.small[
Animation by [Garrick Aden-Buie](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain)
]

---

## Inner join example

.pull-left[
.center[
```{r superhero-table 2, echo = F}
heroes %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.center[
```{r publishers-table-2, echo = F}
publishers %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]
<br>
```{r inber-join-display, eval = F}
heroes %>% 
  inner_join(publishers, by = "Publisher")
```

```{r inner-join-result, echo = F}
heroes %>% 
  inner_join(publishers, by = "Publisher") %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```

---

## Left join

> All rows from `x`, and all columns from `x` and `y`. Rows in `x` with no match in `y` will have `NA` values in the new columns.

```{r left-gif, echo=FALSE, out.width="45%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/blob/main/images/left-join.gif?raw=true")
```
.small[
Animation by [Garrick Aden-Buie](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain)
]

---

## Left join example

.pull-left[
.center[
```{r superhero-table-3, echo = F}
heroes %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.center[
```{r publishers-table-3, echo = F}
publishers %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]
<br>
.small[
```{r left-join-display, eval = F}
heroes %>% 
  left_join(publishers, by = "Publisher")
```
]
```{r left-join-result, echo = F}
heroes %>% 
  left_join(publishers, by = "Publisher") %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```

---

## Right join

> All rows from y, and all columns from `x` and `y`. Rows in `y` with no match in `x` will have `NA` values in the new columns.

```{r right-gif, echo=FALSE, out.width="45%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/blob/main/images/right-join.gif?raw=true")
```
.small[
Animation by [Garrick Aden-Buie](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain)
]

---

## Right join example

.pull-left[
.center[
```{r superhero-table-4, echo = F}
heroes %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.center[
```{r publishers-table-4, echo = F}
publishers %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]
<br>
.small[
```{r right-join-display, eval = F}
heroes %>% 
  right_join(publishers, by = "Publisher")
```
]
```{r right-join-result, echo = F}
heroes %>% 
  right_join(publishers, by = "Publisher") %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```

---

## Full join

> All rows and all columns from both `x` and `y`. Where there are not matching values, returns `NA` for the one missing.

```{r full-gif, echo=FALSE, out.width="45%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/blob/main/images/full-join.gif?raw=true")
```
.small[
Animation by [Garrick Aden-Buie](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain)
]

---

## Full join example

.pull-left[
.center[
```{r superhero-table-5, echo = F}
heroes %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.center[
```{r publishers-table-5, echo = F}
publishers %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]
<br>
.small[
```{r full-join-display, eval = F}
heroes %>% 
  full_join(publishers, by = "Publisher")
```
]
```{r full-join-result, echo = F}
heroes %>% 
  full_join(publishers, by = "Publisher") %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 9)
```

---

## Filtering joins

> Filtering joins .highlight[match observations in the same way as mutating joins], but .highlight[affect the observations, not the variables] ([R for Data Science](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/relational-data.html#filtering-joins)).

- `semi_join()`  

- `anti_join()`

---

## Semi join

> All rows from `x` where there are matching values in `y`, keeping just columns from `x`.

```{r semi-gif, echo=FALSE, out.width="45%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/blob/main/images/semi-join.gif?raw=true")
```
.small[
Animation by [Garrick Aden-Buie](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain)
]

---

## Semi join example

.pull-left[
.center[
```{r superhero-table-6, echo = F}
heroes %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.center[
```{r publishers-table-6, echo = F}
publishers %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]
<br>
```{r semi-join-display, eval = F}
heroes %>% 
  semi_join(publishers, by = "Publisher")
```

```{r semi-join-result, echo = F}
heroes %>% 
  semi_join(publishers, by = "Publisher") %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```

---

## Anti join

> All rows from `x` where there are not matching values in `y`, keeping just columns from `x`.

```{r anti-gif, echo=FALSE, out.width="45%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/blob/main/images/anti-join.gif?raw=true")
```
.small[
Animation by [Garrick Aden-Buie](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain)
]

---

## Anti join example

.pull-left[
.center[
```{r superhero-table-7, echo = F}
heroes %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.center[
```{r publishers-table-7, echo = F}
publishers %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```
]
]
<br>
```{r anti-join-display, eval = F}
heroes %>% 
  anti_join(publishers, by = "Publisher")
```

```{r anti-join-result, echo = F}
heroes %>% 
  anti_join(publishers, by = "Publisher") %>%
  kable("html") %>% 
  kable_styling(font_size = 10)
```

---

## Expanding the joy of joins

Even more join operations (including fuzzy joins) are possible with the [`powerjoin` package](https://github.com/moodymudskipper/powerjoin).



---

## Writing loops

R is very good at performing repetitive tasks. If we want a set of operations to be repeated several times we use what’s known as a loop. When you create a loop, R will execute the instructions in the loop a specified number of times or until a specified condition is met. There are three main types of loop in R: the for loop, the while loop and the repeat loop. Loops are one of the staples of all programming languages, not just R, and can be a powerful tool. 

While you can do both, loops are more often used to loop over rows than over columns.


---


## Writing a for loop

Looping through a vector 

```{r loops}
names_vector <- c("Max", "Tina", "Lindsey", "Anton", "Sharon")


for(i in names_vector) {
  print(paste("The name", i, "consists of", nchar(i), "characters."))
}
```


---

## Writing a for loop

Looping through a dataframe 

```{r loops2}
people_dataframe <- data.frame(
  name = c("Joy", "Juliya", "Boby", "Marry"),
  age = c(78, 12, 22, 55)
)

people_dataframe$age_next_year <- NA

for (i in 1:length(people_dataframe$age_next_year)) {
  people_dataframe$age_next_year[i] <- people_dataframe$age[i] +1
}
print(people_dataframe)
```

---

## Writing a for loop

Looping through a dataframe with a condition

```{r loops3}

people_dataframe$status <- NA

# Loop through the dataframe 
for (i in 1:length(people_dataframe$status)){
  
  if(people_dataframe$age[i] > 67){
  people_dataframe$status[i] <- "retired"
  } else if (people_dataframe$age[i] <= 67 & people_dataframe$age[i] >= 18){
  people_dataframe$status[i] <- "working"
  } else 
  people_dataframe$status[i] <- "child"
}
  
print(people_dataframe)
```

---


## Writing (your own) functions

So far we have looked at functions from a user perspective.

One of the great advantages of R is there are so many built-in functions, and so many additional functions that come with packages. So, one might ask: why would you bother writing your own functions? It makes your code neater and easier to understand for you and others – and as a result, easier to debug!

---

## Writing (your own) functions

```{r functions}
add_three <- function(x){
y <- x + 3
return(y)
}

add_three(5)
```

---


## Writing your own `R` packages

.pull-left[
At a certain point (not now!), you may want to consider writing your own `R` package
- useful for creating reproducible code
- great for distributing your work to others
- for example, we created an [`R` package](https://stefanjuenger.github.io/woRkshoptools/) to facilitate working on our workshop materials
]

.pull-right[
```{r r-packages, echo = FALSE}
woRkshoptools::include_picture("r_packages.jpg")
```
[Read the book here!](https://r-pkgs.org/)
]

---


## It's straightforward in `RStudio`

```{r new-package, echo = FALSE, out.width = "75%"}
woRkshoptools::include_picture("new_package.png")
```
